title: Your Circle of Competence Is Smaller Than You Think

source.original: If you want to be early to a trend, you have to go really deep and be an expert – Parker

body_html:
Warren Buffett and Charlie Munger popularized the concept of a "circle of competence"—the idea that you should operate primarily in domains where you have genuine, tested understanding. It sounds simple, even obvious. Yet in practice, almost everyone overestimates the size of their circle, and this overestimation is one of the most expensive mistakes in investing.

The reason is partly psychological. Exposure to information feels like understanding. Reading ten articles about a sector creates a sense of familiarity that masquerades as expertise. But there is an enormous gap between knowing the narrative around a trend and understanding the mechanics well enough to identify when the narrative is wrong, premature, or missing critical context.

Intellectual honesty about the boundaries of your competence is a genuine competitive advantage. It prevents you from making bets in areas where you have no real edge, and it redirects your energy toward domains where your knowledge can compound into something genuinely differentiating. The willingness to say "I don't know this well enough" is rare and valuable.

This principle applies beyond investing. Entrepreneurs who build in spaces they deeply understand consistently outperform those who chase market trends. Analysts who specialize produce better work than generalists. The pattern is universal: depth of understanding in a focused area generates better outcomes than shallow familiarity across many areas.

Practically, this means regularly auditing your own competence with uncomfortable honesty. For every position you hold or trend you're tracking, ask: could I explain the core dynamics to a skeptic without referencing anyone else's analysis? If not, you're outside your circle—and the market is full of people who are inside theirs, ready to take the other side of your trade.
